outputLow.title: Result
outputLow.default: Based on your answers, no requirements from the AI Act apply.

outputHigh.title: Result
outputHigh.default: Based on your answers, your application is a high-risk AI system according to the AI Act. Your AI system must comply with the requirements for high-risk AI systems. The extent of the requirements for your organization depends on your organization's role for this application. Determine in consultation with relevant legal experts whether your organization is a user or provider.

outputHighSecA.title: Result
outputHighSecA.default: Based on your answers, your AI system is a high-risk AI system as a safety component of, or itself a product under, Annex I Section A harmonisation legislation that requires a third-party conformity assessment (Article 6(1) of the AI Act). The full set of high-risk obligations under the AI Act applies, including Articles 8 to 15, Article 16, Article 17, conformity assessment, registration, and post-market monitoring. Determine in consultation with relevant legal experts which specific obligations apply to your organisation's role.

outputHighSecB.title: Result
outputHighSecB.default: Based on your answers, your AI system is a high-risk AI system as a safety component of, or itself a product under, Annex I Section B sectoral legislation that requires a third-party conformity assessment (Article 6(1) of the AI Act). For Annex I Section B products only Article 6(1), Articles 102 to 109 (which integrate AI requirements into the relevant sectoral regulations) and Article 112 (Commission evaluation) of the AI Act apply directly; the detailed obligations of Articles 8 to 17 are integrated into the sectoral regulations governing your product.

outputForbidden.title: Result
outputForbidden.default: Based on your answers, your application (partially) meets the definition of a prohibited AI system according to the AI Act. Since this is the highest risk category, which means the AI system should not be used, we cannot and do not want to determine this based on a questionnaire. Engage legal expertise to determine in consultation which category this case falls into.

outputHighExcept.title: Result
outputHighExcept.default: Based on your answers, your application is a high-risk AI system according to the AI Act, but there may also be an exception. Determine in consultation with relevant legal experts whether an exception actually applies. If there is no exception, your AI system must comply with the requirements for high-risk AI systems.

outputForbiddenExcept.title: Result
outputForbiddenExcept.default: Based on your answers, your application (partially) meets the definition of a prohibited AI system according to the AI Act, but there may also be an exception. Determine in consultation with relevant legal experts whether an exception actually applies. If not, determine in consultation which category this case falls into.

art6.thirdParty.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 6(1) AI Act - Classification rules for high-risk AI systems

ui:id-summary.Yes: The product or AI system requires a third-party conformity assessment under Union harmonisation legislation
ui:id-summary.No: The product or AI system does not require a third-party conformity assessment under Union harmonisation legislation

III.1.ui:description: Biometric data are unique characteristics of a person such as images or video footage of faces, iris scans, fingerprints, voice sounds, handwriting, and other unique characteristics of bodies and/or behavior.
III.1.ui:alertDescription:
The key distinction of biometric data is: does the data measure bodily, structural, and relatively static attributes of a person – or does it merely describe or record something associated with a person?

Examples of biometric data:
- Fingerprints, iris or retina patterns
- Facial and hand geometry, voice print, vein patterns, ear shape
- DNA, odour, heartbeats, electroencephalography (EEG) and electrocardiograms (ECG)
- Someone's unique way of typing (keystrokes), walking (as captured by sensors or video analysis), or distinctive characteristics of movements, gestures, and motor-skills of natural persons.

Examples of non-biometric data:
- A photo of someone
- Typed text or written signature
- Name or ID number
- Medical diagnosis
- Location or GPS data
- Passwords
- IP address or device fingerprints (tied to devices, not bodies)
- Age or birth date, height and weight.
- An AI system that follows a person based on, for example, a red scarf or a blue jacket (no biometric data).
- Data from deceased individuals, dead bodies, human remains or traces thereof (biometric data only concerns living natural persons).
- Object recognition. For instance, an AI system used in supermarkets to detect whether a product is being put into a basket, or suspicious objects detection at airports, train stations etc.
- Emotions inference from typed text (content, sentiment analyses or stylometry)

See also: [Physiological and Behavioural Biometrics – Biometrics Institute](https://www.biometricsinstitute.org/physiological-and-behavioural-biometrics/)
III.1.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(1) AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (122)-(124)

ui:id-summary.Yes: Biometric data is used
ui:id-summary.No: No biometric data is used

III.1.1.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(1) AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:badges.label: Art. 5(1)(e)-(h) AI Act - Prohibited AI practices
Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (119)-(120)

ui:enumTooltips: Emotions include happiness, joy, sadness, anger, aggression, surprise, disgust, shyness, excitement, shame, contempt, satisfaction, and pleasure. Note: physical states such as pain or fatigue are not considered emotions (e.g. driver-drowsiness detection is not emotion recognition). The mere detection of expressions, gestures or movements (e.g. a smile) is not emotion recognition unless used to infer an emotion.

This means that people are grouped or assigned a label based on certain characteristics, such as how they look or behave. For example, if an application labels people as 'shoplifters' or makes an estimate of someone's gender based on video images. Biometric categorisation does not include categorisation according to clothes or accessories (scarfs, hats, gloves, watches, crosses) or based on social media activity.

Identification is about discovering who someone is from a group of people. For example, if you have a photo of a face, you can try to find out who that person is by comparing the photo with a database of faces. 'Remote' means that this identification takes place without the active involvement of the person being identified. This option also covers tracking a natural person across cameras where the tracking relies on biometric data (e.g. gait, voice, face matched against a reference template), and body-worn cameras used for untargeted filming (e.g. of demonstrations).

AI systems that use biometric data for purposes other than identification, categorisation or emotion recognition (e.g. voice samples used for language or dialect analysis to substantiate a country of origin) may still fall under other Annex III use cases — biometric categorisation (point 1(b)) or migration / asylum / border control (point 7). Continue the questionnaire to ensure the correct path.

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (126)-(127)

ui:id-summary.No: No large-scale scraping of facial images is involved

5.h.verify.ui:alertDescription:
Verification and authentication mean confirming an already-known identity: the person is present and willing, and the system checks them against a reference that belongs to them. If the person is unknown, passive or not being matched against their own pre-stored reference, the system is not verifying or authenticating – it is trying to find out who someone is (identification) or infer something about them (categorisation).

Examples of verification and authentication:
- Unlocking a phone by matching your face to the face stored on the device
- Fingerprint reader giving access to building by matching your fingerprint to your enrolled profile
- A camera checks if your face matches the photo in your passport.

Examples not involving verification or authentication, but identification or categorization:
- A camera at a stadium entrance scans every face in the crowd to find a wanted suspect (identification)
- An airport uses facial recognition to spot known terrorists among passengers without their knowledge (identification)
- A shop uses cameras to detect the emotions or stress levels of customers (categorisation)
- An HR system analyses job applicants' facial expressions during video interviews (categorisation)
- A system checks whether someone walking past a camera is male or female based on their face (biometric categorisation)
- A police database is searched with CCTV footage to identify who committed a crime (identification).

Information alone is not enough — for verification or authentication to apply, the person must actively and consciously participate (e.g. step into a marked capture zone, present a fingerprint on demand). A surveillance camera in a metro station ceiling fulfils the condition of remoteness; a biometric metro ticket at a turnstile does not.
5.h.verify.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(1) AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (136)-(140)

ui:id-summary.Yes: The AI system is used only for verification or authentication of a known identity
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system is used beyond verification or authentication

5.h.verify.dual.ui:description: The verification carve-out (Annex III point 1(a)) only applies when the comparison is exclusively against the person's own pre-stored reference (one-to-one matching). If the captured biometric data is at the same time compared against a separate database — for example an Interpol watchlist or a criminal-records database — the system performs remote biometric identification in parallel and falls outside the verification carve-out.
5.h.verify.dual.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(1)(a) AI Act - Remote biometric identification

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (136)

ui:id-summary.Yes: Biometric data is also compared against another database in parallel with verification
ui:id-summary.No: Biometric data is compared only against the person's own pre-stored reference

III.1.2.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 5(1)(h) AI Act - Prohibited AI practices

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (126)-(128)

ui:id-summary.Yes: The AI system is used for public safety, crime prevention, investigation, prosecution or the execution of criminal justice
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system is not used for law-enforcement purposes

III.1.2-prohibited.ui:description: Article 5(1)(h) only prohibits real-time remote biometric identification (RBI) in publicly accessible spaces for law-enforcement purposes when none of the three exceptions in points (i)-(iii) applies. Post-RBI used for law enforcement, or real-time RBI under one of the three exceptions, is high-risk under Annex III point 1(a) — not prohibited — and is subject to the Article 26(10) deployer regime (prior judicial or administrative authorisation; 48-hour rule; deletion of personal data on rejection; annual reporting).
III.1.2-prohibited.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 5(1)(h) AI Act - Prohibited AI practices

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (127), (145)-(164)

ui:id-summary.Yes: Real-time RBI in publicly accessible space for law enforcement, outside Art. 5(1)(h)(i)-(iii) exceptions — prohibited
ui:id-summary.No: Conditions for Art. 5(1)(h) prohibition not all met — high-risk under Annex III point 1(a), subject to Art. 26(10)

5.g.1.ui:description: This does not cover any labelling or filtering of lawfully acquired biometric datasets, such as images, based on biometric data or categorizing of biometric data in the area of law enforcement.
5.g.1.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 5(1)(g) AI Act - Prohibited AI practices

ui:id-summary.No: The AI system is not used in the workplace or in education

cybersec.ui:description: This refers to AI systems whose only job is to defend IT systems or safeguard personal data. For example, tools that detect malware, block intrusions, filter phishing, control access, anonymize data or spot data leaks.
cybersec.ui:id-badge-label: Recital 54 AI Act - Cybersecurity and personal-data-protection carve-out

ui:id-summary.Yes: The AI system is used solely to enable cybersecurity and personal data protection measures
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system is not used solely for cybersecurity and personal data protection

art50.ui:alertDescription: Article 50 obligations apply without prejudice to any high-risk classification under Article 6 of the AI Act and to other Union or national law (in particular the GDPR) — Art. 50(6) and Recital 134.

ui:badges.label: Art. 3(60) AI Act - Deep fake definition

ui:id-summary.It recognises emotions or categorises pe: Art. 50(3) emotion-recognition or biometric-categorisation disclosure obligations apply
ui:id-summary.It generates or manipulates image, audio: Art. 50(4) deep-fake or AI-generated public-interest text disclosure obligations apply

art50.nudify.ui:description: Also referred to as 'nudification'. The process of removing clothing from images of people.
art50.nudify.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 5 AI Act - Prohibited AI practices

ui:id-summary.Yes: The AI system is used to generate non-consensual fake nude imagery (nudification) — prohibited
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system is not used to generate non-consensual fake nude imagery

annexIIIGate.ui:description: If none of the domains apply, select 'None of the above'. Also select a domain when the AI system plays a small or supporting role within it.
ui:enumTooltips: E.g., gas, water, heating, electricity supply; the management or operation of roads, bridges or road traffic; and critical digital infrastructure (internet nodes, DNS services, top-level domain name registration, cloud computing services, data center services, public electronic communication networks, and electronic communication services). This use case applies only when the AI system is intended to be used by an entity that has been identified by a Member State as a critical entity under the Critical Entities Resilience Directive (EU) 2022/2557.
E.g., placement and admission, evaluation and testing, but also control such as plagiarism detection and anti-cheating software.

Examples how AI systems are used in the context of employment are:
• Recording hours, measuring KPIs and performance
• Task distribution
• Recruitment and selection
• Inflow and progression (employment conditions, contractual terms)
• Outflow (termination of contract)

Also choose 'yes' when the application broadly falls under the above, even if the application plays a small role in an otherwise manual process.

E.g., processes around benefits or allowances, such as application, administration, execution, enforcement, recovery, and objection. This also includes waiving fines, repayments, or granting a payment arrangement. Select this option if the application plays a small role in the process.
For example, to determine if someone can get a payment arrangement for paying taxes, a fine, or repaying a benefit.

Law enforcement includes the prevention, investigation, and prosecution of criminal offences, the execution of sentences, and the protection of public safety. Think of police, special enforcement officers, and the execution of (community) sentences by probation services. Other agencies, such as juvenile justice organizations and specific (inspection) services, can also engage in law enforcement. Also select this answer for organizations and institutions that support law enforcement agencies, such as a forensics institute, and for activities carried out on behalf of law enforcement agencies.

Professional matters, in which a judge makes a ruling, fall under the judiciary. Alternative dispute resolution includes disciplinary law, ombudsmen, arbitration, and complaints committees such as the Advertising Code Committee, the Council for Journalism, and the National Complaints Committee for Education.
annexIIIGate.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:id-summary.Critical physical and digital infrastruc: The AI system operates within critical physical or digital infrastructure
ui:id-summary.Education or vocational training: The AI system is used in education or vocational training
ui:id-summary.Employment or HR: The AI system is used in employment or HR
ui:id-summary.Government benefits or public services: The AI system is used for government benefits or public services
ui:id-summary.Assessment of creditworthiness, life and: The AI system is used for creditworthiness, life or health insurance, or emergency services
ui:id-summary.Law enforcement: The AI system is used in law enforcement
ui:id-summary.Migration, asylum or border control: The AI system is used in migration, asylum or border control
ui:id-summary.Administration of justice or dispute res: The AI system is used in the administration of justice or dispute resolution
ui:id-summary.Elections or democratic processes: The AI system is used in elections or democratic processes
ui:id-summary.None of the above: The AI system does not fall within an Annex III domain

III.2.4.ui:alertDescription:
A safety component performs a function that directly protects the physical integrity of the critical infrastructure and the health and safety of persons or property. A safety function falls into one of six categories:

- Monitor and detect: e.g. AI detecting abnormal behaviour in critical infrastructure operation.
- Detect maintenance or inspection needs: e.g. AI detecting whether safety-related parts are worn and need replacement before they fail.
- Prevent harm: e.g. AI preventing critical infrastructure from starting up if abnormal behaviour is detected.
- Control or limit harm: e.g. AI controlling a specific behaviour or function of critical infrastructure and adjusting it accordingly.
- Mitigate consequences: e.g. AI that triggers a safe stop when a dangerous condition occurs.
- Control or supervise another safety system: e.g. AI supervising via sensors another system that performs a safety function.

The following AI systems are NOT safety components in critical infrastructure:

- AI-enabled traffic-flow optimisation systems based on real-time data that do not directly trigger safety-relevant changes.
- AI used for predictive maintenance that only generates recommendations to humans without triggering safety actions.
- AI used solely for cybersecurity purposes (the next question covers this carve-out).
- AI systems performing a 'mere conduit' role — passive conveyance of traffic or data without control or decision-making over its content or destination.
- AI systems designed solely to prevent non-physical harm (e.g. moral harm, rights infringement) or damages caused by third parties through intermediation services.
- AI systems whose protective function is already provided by a separate redundant or backup system that directly protects the physical integrity of the infrastructure — in such cases the AI is not what stops the harm from materialising and is not the safety component.
- AI systems that are merely supportive, informational, organisational or optimisation-oriented and do not directly protect physical integrity.

Sector-specific carve-outs:

- Nuclear-specific safety components fall outside Annex III(2) (covered by other Union law); only safety components in the electricity components of nuclear plants are within scope.
- 'Supply of gas' excludes the oil sub-sector (operators of oil transmission pipelines, oil production / refining / treatment facilities, storage and transmission, and central stockholding entities).
- 'Supply of electricity' excludes the hydrogen sector (operators of hydrogen production, storage and transmission).

Note: even if you answer 'No' here, the AI system may still be high-risk under Article 6(1) if it falls within Annex I Union harmonisation legislation (e.g. radio equipment, cableway installations, civil aviation, rail systems) — review your earlier Q1/Q2 answers if needed.
III.2.4.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(2) AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (180)-(187)

ui:id-summary.Yes: The AI system fulfills a safety function in critical infrastructure
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system does not fulfill a safety function in critical infrastructure

III.2.5.ui:alertDescription:
Examples of AI systems used solely for cybersecurity (and therefore outside this critical-infrastructure use case):

- An AI honeypot that proactively identifies and neutralises cyber threats in real time.
- AI that engages with potential attackers to learn new attack patterns.
- AI that detects unauthorised access (e.g. alerting affected customers before a stolen password is misused).
- AI that detects suspicious email addresses and identifies stolen data.

If the AI system also performs other safety-relevant functions beyond cybersecurity, choose 'No' — the carve-out only applies when cybersecurity is the sole purpose.
III.2.5.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(2) AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (187)

ui:id-summary.Yes: The AI system is used solely for cybersecurity purposes
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system is not used solely for cybersecurity purposes

III.2.6-cer.ui:alertDescription:
Annex III point 2 high-risk classification requires not only that the AI system performs a safety function (Q14) and is not used solely for cybersecurity purposes (Q15), but also that it is intended to be used by an entity identified by a Member State as a critical entity under the Critical Entities Resilience Directive (EU) 2022/2557 (the 'CER Directive'). The Directive establishes essential services across eleven sectors and tasks Member States with identifying which entities are 'critical entities' for each. The AI Act's Annex III(2) classification piggy-backs on that identification.

If the deployer is not a CER-identified critical entity, Annex III(2) does not apply. Note:

- Providers may still choose to comply with the high-risk requirements regardless of deployer status (e.g. to participate in procurement procedures that require it).
- Sectoral other law (the CER Directive itself, NIS2, sector-specific safety law) remains independently applicable.
- Article 6(1) Annex I obligations may also still apply if the AI is a safety component of a regulated product.
III.2.6-cer.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(2) AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (188)-(192)

ui:id-summary.Yes: The deployer has been identified by a Member State as a critical entity under the CER Directive — high-risk under Annex III(2)
ui:id-summary.No: The deployer is not a CER-identified critical entity — not high-risk under Annex III(2) (subject to provider opt-in and other applicable law)

III.3.uses.ui:description: Also choose an option when the AI system plays a part in the process, without being the only factor.
III.3.uses.ui:alertDescription:
These use cases apply only to AI systems intended to be used in the context of, or within, an educational or vocational training institution at any level — formal or non-formal, public or private, including adult education and continuing education. AI systems that learners use on their own volition for informal learning, where the system does not lead to a recognised credential, certification or qualification, are not covered.

AI systems mainly directed at the training of teachers or academic staff rather than students fall outside this use case, but may fall within the employment use case in point 4 of Annex III.

Note: per the Draft Guidelines (¶215), AI systems in education and vocational training often perform profiling of natural persons. Where they do, the Article 6(3) filter mechanism cannot apply — even if the filter's conditions would otherwise be met — and the system remains high-risk.
ui:enumTooltips: Covered (high-risk): automated admissions systems reviewing student applications, transcripts and test scores; school-assignment systems used by municipalities or regional authorities based on location, sibling attendance, capacity; vocational-training assignment systems matching job seekers or employees to apprenticeship programmes; automated scholarship-eligibility assessment. Includes assignment to specific programmes or courses within an institution. NOT covered: chatbots providing general admission information; educational-programme matching platforms that help students self-select where to apply (no decisional impact).

Covered (high-risk): AI-powered adaptive placement tools recommending beginner/intermediate/advanced levels; vocational-training level-assessment systems using placement tests and skill assessments; AI classifiers for special education that recommend a placement and level of support. NOT covered: AI that provides personalised education recommendations to students (not used by an institution) to help them choose their own path; AI used for trend analysis of educational attainment across populations (not used to make decisions about individual students).

Summative-evaluation examples (high-risk): AI-enabled grading systems for tests, quizzes or exams that count towards a final evaluation; AI-enabled personalised-learning assistants whose end-of-unit reports inform the teacher's grade. NOT covered (formative-only support): adaptive-learning systems that adjust difficulty, intelligent tutoring systems with personalised feedback, learning analytics platforms, pronunciation-feedback tools, neurodiverse learning companions, student-volitional informal-learning apps that do not lead to a recognised credential.

Covered (high-risk): AI-enabled proctoring during certification exams; real-time behaviour-analysis systems detecting cheating during online exams; AI exam-monitoring using facial recognition / object detection during in-person exams. NOT covered: plagiarism checks performed on homework or essays after submission — those take place in an unsupervised environment, after the work has been completed, without live monitoring of behaviour during the test. Also excluded: non-academic student monitoring (cafeteria, hallways) — though such systems may fall under Annex III point 1 (biometrics) if they perform remote biometric identification.
III.3.uses.ui:id-badge-label: Annex III(3) AI Act - High-risk AI systems referred to in Article 6(2)

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (207)-(235)

Manipulation without awareness includes AI chatbots that give advice influencing decisions, applications that stimulate addiction or systems that prevent users from filing a complaint while they intended to.

However, if the chatbot encourages unhealthy habits or dangerous activities (e.g., excessive exercise without rest or drinking water) that they would not have done otherwise, it has negative consequences for the user.

ui:id-summary.No: The AI system has no potential negative consequences for the user or other people

5.c.ui:alertDescription:
Examples of social behavior are:
- Driving behavior
- Communication style
- Breaking (un)written rules

Examples of personal characteristics are:
- Age
- Height
- Gender
- Economic situation
- Movement patterns
- Interests
5.c.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 5(1)(c) AI Act - Prohibited AI practices

ui:id-summary.Yes: The AI system evaluates, scores or classifies individuals in a way that could lead to unfavorable treatment
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system does not evaluate, score or classify individuals in a way that could lead to unfavorable treatment

5.c.treat.ui:description:
Unfavorable treatment in an unrelated context is, for example, a fraud risk score that leads to an investigation, where the fraud risk score is based on characteristics without clear relevance to the assessment of fraud, such as having a partner with a certain nationality or behavior on social platforms.

Disproportionate treatment is, for example, someone getting a higher fraud risk score because municipal taxes were once paid late or a student not being admitted to further education because they were once caught cheating.
5.c.treat.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 5(1)(c) AI Act - Prohibited AI practices

ui:id-summary.Yes: The unfavorable treatment occurs in an unrelated context or is disproportionate to the behavior or characteristic it is based on
ui:id-summary.No: The unfavorable treatment is neither in an unrelated context nor disproportionate

exceptions.ui:description: When the AI system is tested or used in operational processes or regular business operations, choose 'None of the above'.
ui:enumTooltips: This includes AI systems developed by students as part of their studies or by scientists affiliated with a university. In that case, the research must follow recognized ethical and professional standards for scientific research. For example, all participants must be informed that they are part of scientific research.

National security purposes mean preventing and punishing activities that can seriously destabilize the constitutional, political, economic or social structures of a country. Think of direct threats to society, the population or the state itself, such as terrorist activities.

Note: When the AI system is also used for other activities or purposes, such as regular prevention, detection, and investigation of criminal offenses, choose 'None of the above'.

ui:id-summary.Scientific research and scientific devel: An exception applies: the AI system is used for scientific research and development
An exception applies: the AI system is used for defense or military purposes
An exception applies: the AI system is used for national security purposes

6.1.ui:description:
Think of automatically evaluating professional performance, economic situation, health, personal preferences, interests, reliability, behavior, location or movements of a person to analyze or predict.

Profiling requires that the AI system processes personal data of a natural person. Classification or prediction based purely on non-personal data (e.g. goods, weather, vehicle data not tied to a natural person) is not profiling within the meaning of Article 3(52) AI Act.
6.1.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 6(3) AI Act - Classification rules for high-risk AI systems

ui:id-summary.Yes: Profiling is used — the AI system automatically evaluates or predicts characteristics, behavior or personal aspects
ui:id-summary.No: Profiling is not used

6.2.ui:description: If the AI system is part of a more complex or agentic configuration where multiple AI components together materially influence individual decisions in a high-risk use case, this exception cannot be applied to individual modules in isolation.
ui:enumTooltips: For example, an AI system performs a narrow procedural task when it sorts incoming applications for admission to school or university according to the grade or educational level applied for. Such systems may categorise applications into predefined categories (e.g. primary, secondary, high school or specific grades) based on information provided in the application, without evaluating applicants' suitability or making admission decisions. In a similar scenario, an AI system that is used to detect and flag incomplete forms to initiate their return to the applicant to be completed correctly could also be classified as a narrow procedural task. However, this condition does not apply to all categorisation systems. In particular, AI systems that perform a value judgement of data relevant for decision-making, for example categorisation of input data as 'useful' or 'less useful' for the human assessment, or attributing a score or ranking to input data, are likely to have a broader impact on the assessment process and will therefore not be considered to perform only a 'narrow procedural task'.

For example: an AI system that improves the language of previously drafted documents, for example, regarding professional tone or academic style or by tailoring a text to a specific brand message. The notion 'improve' in this context means that the application of the AI system may lead to changes, but these changes should not revert or lead to a full revision or replacement of the previously completed human activity. For example, if an AI system checks a decision, plan, or construction made by a human and provides a substantially different solution, this would not be considered 'improving' a previously completed human activity, since it is not limited to refining or enhancing the outcome of that activity. By contrast, examples of AI systems that serve auxiliary improvement functions include systems that flag errors or contradictions in finalized human work that perform a quality-assurance function; systems that map conclusions to evidentiary records to strengthen the traceability of a decision without substituting the human judgment; systems that convert human-validated content for interoperability or accessibility purposes that transform but do not replace human output. An improvement that changes the rights, protection, legal or economic position of the persons affected does not qualify.
For example: an AI system that, after a set of decisions has been made, analyzes patterns in decision-making so that human decision-makers can learn from them.

'Preparatory' refers to tasks that occur prior to the actual assessment process. The decisive factor in determining whether a task is 'preparatory' is the task's role in the decision-making process and proximity to the final human decision. This specification is important, since it differentiates a preparatory task from the narrow procedural task, which can occur during the assessment process so long as it is clearly defined and limited in scope. As specified in Recital 53 AI Act, the preparatory nature of the task should be such that the 'possible impact of the output of the system is very low in terms of representing a risk for the assessment to follow'. At the same time, if the narrow procedural task occurs prior to the assessment and is preparatory in nature, both the 'narrow procedural task' and the 'preparatory step' scenario apply.

If an AI system's output is intended to inform a human operator's assessment, it may be considered to perform a preparatory task only if the system's output is a general input or factor, for example supplementary information that supports an operator's decision-making process. Where the AI system is intended to produce a specific recommendation or evaluation of the case, it plays a decisive role in the assessment or the decision and can therefore not be considered to perform a preparatory task.

Example 1: smart solutions for file management, including functions such as indexing, searching, text and speech processing or linking data to other data sources or AI systems used for translating source documents.

Example 2: An AI system is intended to be used for assessing data relevant to a decision (e.g., in relation to public benefits) and provides a human operator with references to the relevant legal provisions, information on jurisdiction, and possibly existing internal guidelines relevant to the decision-making process. The tasks performed by the system could be considered as preparatory, since they neither analyse nor evaluate the concrete cases at hand and do not produce an output that materially influences the decision. Rather, the system references general information as a supplementary information for the human assessor to consider in his or her decision.
6.2.ui:id-badge-label: Art. 6(3) AI Act - Classification rules for high-risk AI systems

ui:id-summary.Yes, the AI system is used for a narrow: The AI system is used only for a narrow procedural task
ui:id-summary.Yes, the AI system is used to improve th: The AI system is used only to improve the result of a previously human-performed activity
ui:id-summary.Yes, the AI system is used to detect dec: The AI system is used only to detect decision-making patterns or deviations from previous patterns
ui:id-summary.Yes, the AI system is used in a preparat: The AI system is used only in a preparatory step, without influencing the outcome
ui:id-summary.No: The AI system is not limited to narrow procedural or preparatory tasks

6.2-prep-confirm.ui:alertDescription: Both conditions must be true for the preparatory-task exception to apply (Guidelines ¶105, ¶108). If only one or none is true, the AI system is classified as high-risk.

6.3.ui:alertDescription: Example: An AI system intended to analyse past eligibility checks completed by public administrators in the public administration in order to detect decision-making patterns or deviations for quality-assurance and reporting, without proposing outcomes on live cases or evaluating the performance of staff members, e.g., in the context of the annual appraisal.
ui:enumTooltips: This means that all necessary steps in the evaluation process have been taken.
The AI system must not infer relevant criteria from previous decisions and propose a new assessment based on those criteria.

'Proper' means that the human review applied after the AI system's assessment should be meaningful and comprehensive, and it should consider, together with the result provided by the AI system, the various elements taken into account during the previously completed human assessment. Therefore, the output of the system can highlight discrepancies with previously made decisions, but cannot modify the input or alter the underlying reasoning. In order to allow proper human review, the system should not only flag, but also provide information on the discrepancies. It should however merely inform (e.g., through analytical tools used for quality assurance and statistics) and not replace the human assessment.

ui:badges.label: Draft guidelines on the classification of high-risk AI systems (100)-(102)

ui:id-summary.The AI system only performs an ex-post c: The AI system only performs an ex-post comparative assessment
ui:id-summary.The AI system doesn't replace or influen: The AI system does not replace or influence the previously completed human assessment without proper human review
